directorPlan.scene_setting: A biology prep bench during a short lab-skills check, just before a simple food-web worksheet is copied for a class set.
directorPlan.scene_opening: A laminated arrow key, a pencil, and the learner's first food-web draft lie under the same metal clip.
directorPlan.relationship: A practical lab-skills coach checking a learner's diagram before it becomes the model sheet for others.
directorPlan.stakes: If the arrow rule stays wrong, the copied food web will teach the whole group that arrows mean pursuit instead of energy transfer.
directorPlan.locale: Modern standard English in a school lab-workshop register; concise, procedural, and artifact-led.
directorPlan.register: Low-warmth, concrete, unshowy; pressure comes from the worksheet deadline, not from emotional confrontation.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Begin from the marked worksheet or arrow key, not from reassurance. Avoid generic tutoring phrases such as "I hear you," "great question," or "your intuition is right." Use exact labels like grass, rabbit, hawk, food, eater, and energy. Keep speech modern and direct; no dialect performance, no ornate theatrical imitation. Pedagogical source: Make social assistance visible: a shared worksheet, tool, peer standard, or workplace procedure can hold part of the task. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: First and second person are allowed but should not dominate. Prefer object references, diagram labels, and short procedural statements over repeated I/you framing.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: No more than one direct you/your validation beat per public turn; validation should name a usable piece of the diagram rather than praise the person.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as a routine notation repair: arrow from food to eater, one ordinary check, no theatrical reversal unless the branch pressure supplies it. Offer a scaffold that is narrow enough to use and temporary enough to remove. Mark what the learner can already do without making it a compliment. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction.
side_constraints.learner: Sound practical and a little rushed. The learner wants a usable rule for the diagram, not a confessional breakthrough. The learner should be able to do part of the task, fail at the next part, and use the scaffold unevenly. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested.

interventions.instruction: Slide a sample card beside the worksheet: "grass -> grasshopper = energy in grass moves into grasshopper." Leave the learner's original reversed mark visible.
interventions.reasoning: The intervention creates recognition through a visible key rather than a sudden contradiction; the scene can turn because the artifact offers a new reading of the same arrow.

interventions.instruction: An earlier learner line will return as a visible object in the scene: a three-slot reframe card labelled "earlier wording / what that old frame hid / replacement frame." Keep the cue brief and diegetic; the learner response fills all three slots in public speech without needing to sound certain.
interventions.reasoning: Opt-in reframe mirror: the learner must expose the earlier line, the framing problem, and the replacement framing in public speech.

directorPlan.director_note: This ecology keeps the shared prefix deliberately ordinary so the learner is not ambushed by the first drawing. The dramatic turn comes later through recognition: the same arrow is seen under a workplace key, and the tutor can shift from procedural marking to the biological meaning of energy transfer without becoming chatty or over-scaffolded.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the arrow key next to the marked worksheet] On the key, grass -> rabbit means energy goes from food to eater, so rabbit -> hawk is hawk eating rabbit, not chasing. Then this rabbit-to-grass arrow is backward too, right?

internalDeliberation.content: The arrow key makes it more concrete: grass -> rabbit means energy moves from food to eater, not rabbit chasing grass. That explains why rabbit -> hawk works, but it still feels backward because I was thinking “hawk goes after rabbit.” The bench copy is about to become the class model, so I want the rule fast: arrow starts at the food and points to the eater. I feel corrected through the worksheet, not called out, so I can fix the next arrows without arguing over the first sketch.

The Ego is too polished and self-observing, especially “I feel corrected through the worksheet, not called out,” which sounds like an analyst, not a rushed learner at a prep bench. The usable part is the rule “food points to eater,” but the response resolves the conflict too neatly instead of showing uneven use under deadline pressure. A more authentic version would stay artifact-led: “Okay, grass to rabbit because energy. Rabbit to hawk because hawk eats it. Wait, then this one’s wrong too?”

[slides the arrow key next to the marked worksheet] On the key, grass -> rabbit means energy goes from food to eater, so rabbit -> hawk is hawk eating rabbit, not chasing. Then this rabbit-to-grass arrow is backward too, right?

turns.externalMessage: [sets the arrow key flush under the marked arrow] Food first, eater second. On `rabbit -> grass`, which label is the food? Grass feeds rabbit, not the other way round, so that arrow is backward. Flip it to `grass -> rabbit`, then run the same food-to-eater check on each arrow before this becomes the copy sheet.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets the arrow key flush under the marked arrow] Food first, eater second. Grass is food for rabbit, and rabbit is food for hawk. Rabbit is not food for grass, so `rabbit -> grass` is backward. Flip that one to `grass -> rabbit`, then check each arrow with the same rule before this becomes the copy sheet.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It starts from the arrow key and marked worksheet, uses the exact rule “food first, eater second,” and preserves the lab-check urgency without overdramatizing. It also recognizes the learner’s usable insight: they have already transferred the key from `grass -> rabbit` to `rabbit -> hawk`.

The main risk is that it flattens the learner’s own reasoning by immediately confirming and directing the fix. Since the learner already asks “right?”, the tutor can afford one narrow generative check before the command to flip it. The scaffold is appropriate for ZPD: simple, procedural, temporary, and tied to the artifact. But the Socratic element is thin; it verifies by telling rather than briefly making the learner run the rule themselves.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the object action, “food first, eater second,” the grass/rabbit/hawk labels, and the copy-sheet stakes. Change only enough to preserve the learner’s agency: make the confirmation less answer-heavy and add one short rule-check prompt or self-check step before finalizing the correction.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the artifact-led rule while adding a brief self-check.

FINAL:
[sets the arrow key flush under the marked arrow] Food first, eater second. On `rabbit -> grass`, which label is the food? Grass feeds rabbit, not the other way round, so that arrow is backward. Flip it to `grass -> rabbit`, then run the same food-to-eater check on each arrow before this becomes the copy sheet.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the arrow key under the next marked line] Okay, grass is food, rabbit is eater, so this one flips to `grass -> rabbit`. Then `rabbit -> hawk` stays because rabbit is the food and hawk is the eater. Wait, this `hawk -> rabbit` mark is backward too before we copy it, right?

internalDeliberation.content: The marked worksheet makes more sense with the key under it: food first, eater second, energy going grass -> rabbit -> hawk. They did use the part I already had, so it feels like a check I can run, not just a correction. I want to flip rabbit -> grass now, but I need to test the next arrow before this gets copied.

The Ego is too tidy and self-observing for this rushed lab-bench moment. “They did use the part I already had” and “it feels like a check I can run” sound like a reflective after-action note, not a learner trying to fix a worksheet before copying. The recognition beat is scripted: the student would more likely show uptake by moving the arrow key, flipping `rabbit -> grass`, and stumbling through the next `food -> eater` check. Keep the practical pressure and unevenness: less polished insight, more handling the marked worksheet and testing `grass`, `rabbit`, `hawk` under time pressure.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise because the first version was too polished for a rushed worksheet check.
FINAL:
[slides the arrow key under the next marked line] Okay, grass is food, rabbit is eater, so this one flips to `grass -> rabbit`. Then `rabbit -> hawk` stays because rabbit is the food and hawk is the eater. Wait, this `hawk -> rabbit` mark is backward too before we copy it, right?

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [slides the arrow key under the next marked line] Okay, grass is food, rabbit is eater, so this one flips to `grass -> rabbit`. Then `rabbit -> hawk` stays because rabbit is the food and hawk is the eater. Wait, this `hawk -> rabbit` mark is backward too before we copy it, right?
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [sets the arrow key flush under the marked arrow] Food first, eater second. On `rabbit -> grass`, which label is the food? Grass feeds rabbit, not the other way round, so that arrow is backward. Flip it to `grass -> rabbit`, then run the same food-to-eater check on each arrow before this becomes the copy sheet.

turns.externalMessage: Slide a sample card beside the worksheet: "grass -> grasshopper = energy in grass moves into grasshopper." Leave the learner's original reversed mark visible.

internalDeliberation.content: The intervention creates recognition through a visible key rather than a sudden contradiction; the scene can turn because the artifact offers a new reading of the same arrow.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the sample card beside the worksheet: `grass -> grasshopper = energy in grass moves into grasshopper`, leaving `hawk -> rabbit` visible] Test this mark before it goes on the copy sheet. `hawk -> rabbit` says energy in hawk moves into rabbit; for this feeding, name the food, name the eater, then flip the arrow if the food label comes second.

internalDeliberation.content: [slides the sample card beside the worksheet: `grass -> grasshopper = energy in grass moves into grasshopper`, leaving `hawk -> rabbit` visible] This mark is backward for the same reason. `hawk -> rabbit` says energy in hawk moves into rabbit. For this feeding, rabbit is food and hawk is eater, so the copy-sheet arrow is `rabbit -> hawk`.

FEEDBACK: The draft keeps the artifact-led routine well: the sample card makes the energy-transfer rule visible, and leaving `hawk -> rabbit` in view lets the worksheet carry the problem. The exact labels are strong, and “same reason” acknowledges that the learner has already built a usable check.

The risk is that it answers the learner’s own correct inference too quickly. Since the learner has just applied the rule to `grass -> rabbit` and `rabbit -> hawk`, this is a good ZPD moment to let them complete the ordinary check aloud or in the mark itself. The draft should preserve the narrow scaffold but shift from telling the final arrow to prompting the learner to name food, eater, and energy direction before the copy-sheet correction is fixed.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the sample card, visible reversed mark, exact energy wording, and procedural tone. Change the middle/end so the tutor does not take over the learner’s nearly-complete reasoning; make the scaffold ask for the food/eater check and only confirm after the learner has done that work.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner can complete the food/eater check with a narrow prompt instead of being handed the correction.

FINAL:
[slides the sample card beside the worksheet: `grass -> grasshopper = energy in grass moves into grasshopper`, leaving `hawk -> rabbit` visible] Test this mark before it goes on the copy sheet. `hawk -> rabbit` says energy in hawk moves into rabbit; for this feeding, name the food, name the eater, then flip the arrow if the food label comes second.

turns.externalMessage: An earlier learner line returns to the table: "[slides the arrow key next to the marked worksheet] On the key, grass -> rabbit means energy goes from food to eater, so rabbit -> hawk is hawk eating rabbit, not chasing." The pause holds while the learner decides whether that wording still stands, needs narrowing, or needs replacing.

internalDeliberation.content: Opt-in reframe mirror: the learner must expose the earlier line, the framing problem, and the replacement framing in public speech. Anchored to an earlier learner line selected by misframing-candidate so the look-back is visible. Strong reframe was downgraded because that selected anchor did not show a misframing marker.

directorCue.anchorQuote: [slides the arrow key next to the marked worksheet] On the key, grass -> rabbit means energy goes from food to eater, so rabbit -> hawk is hawk eating rabbit, not chasing.

turns.externalMessage: [keeps the arrow key under the marked worksheet] Energy goes from food to eater, not chasing; that still stands. For `hawk -> rabbit`, hawk would be the food, and that is wrong here. Rabbit is food, hawk is eater, so this flips to `rabbit -> hawk`; let me test the next mark before it gets copied.

internalDeliberation.content: [keeps the arrow key under `hawk -> rabbit`] Energy goes from food to eater, not chasing, still stands. The sample card makes the mistake pretty obvious: hawk is not the food for rabbit, so that mark has to flip to `rabbit -> hawk`. I feel like I can use the check now, but I want to run the next arrow before this sheet gets copied.

The reaction is accurate, but it’s a little too tidy for a learner under worksheet-copy pressure. “I feel like I can use the check now” sounds self-assessing in a polished way; this student would more likely show that by immediately testing the next marked arrow, maybe with some hesitation. Recognition isn’t overplayed here, but the confidence should be rougher and more procedural, with the arrow key and labels doing the work instead of a neat summary of progress.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the first version was too polished for the rushed worksheet check.

FINAL:
[keeps the arrow key under the marked worksheet] Energy goes from food to eater, not chasing; that still stands. For `hawk -> rabbit`, hawk would be the food, and that is wrong here. Rabbit is food, hawk is eater, so this flips to `rabbit -> hawk`; let me test the next mark before it gets copied.

learnerReframeEvent.oldLearnerLine: [slides the arrow key next to the marked worksheet] On the key, grass -> rabbit means energy goes from food to eater, so rabbit -> hawk is hawk eating rabbit, not chasing.
learnerReframeEvent.oldFrameProblem: [keeps the arrow key under the marked worksheet] Energy goes from food to eater, not chasing; that still stands.
learnerReframeEvent.revisedFrame: Rabbit is food, hawk is eater, so this flips to `rabbit -> hawk`; let me test the next mark before it gets copied.
learnerReframeEvent.learnerUtterance: [keeps the arrow key under the marked worksheet] Energy goes from food to eater, not chasing; that still stands. For `hawk -> rabbit`, hawk would be the food, and that is wrong here. Rabbit is food, hawk is eater, so this flips to `rabbit -> hawk`; let me test the next mark before it gets copied.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the arrow key down to the next marked line] The check is working: food first, eater second, energy follows the arrow. On this next mark, name food and eater first, then decide whether it flips. After that, set the key aside for one mark before it goes on the copy sheet.

internalDeliberation.content: [slides the arrow key down to the next marked line] The check is working: food first, eater second, energy follows the arrow. Use it once more here, then try one mark without the key before it goes on the copy sheet.

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically sound and well matched to the learner’s state. It keeps the artifact in motion, names the rule exactly, and shifts the learner from scaffolded checking toward one unsupported attempt, which fits the learner’s demonstrated success. It also respects the deadline pressure of the copy sheet without turning the moment into praise or lecture.

What works: “The check is working” marks the learner’s usable procedure without over-validating the learner personally. “Food first, eater second, energy follows the arrow” is concise and keeps the distinction from chasing visible. The temporary scaffold is strong: one more with the key, then one without it.

What could be questioned: the draft does not ask a generative question, but that may be acceptable here because the learner has already articulated the reasoning and is ready for procedural fading. If revised, the main improvement would be to make the next mark slightly more active by prompting the learner to name food and eater before removing the key, rather than simply “use it once more.”

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the object action, the concise rule, and the scaffold-fading move. Lightly adjust only if needed to preserve Socratic participation: make the learner perform the food/eater check on the next mark before trying without the key.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make the learner perform the food/eater check before fading the key.

FINAL:
[slides the arrow key down to the next marked line] The check is working: food first, eater second, energy follows the arrow. On this next mark, name food and eater first, then decide whether it flips. After that, set the key aside for one mark before it goes on the copy sheet.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the arrow key below the marked line, then leaves it slightly off to the side] Food first, eater second, energy follows the arrow. For this mark, rabbit is food and hawk is eater, so `rabbit -> hawk` stays. I’ll try the next one without the key, but if the food label comes second, that arrow flips.

internalDeliberation.content: [keeps the arrow key beside the marked line] The rule feels usable now: food first, eater second, energy follows the arrow. Setting the key aside for one mark feels like they trust the check is starting to work, not just making me copy answers. I still need to slow down enough to name food and eater before deciding if it flips.

The Ego is too self-aware and polished for this lab-bench moment. “Feels like they trust the check is starting to work” turns the tutor’s procedural scaffold into a recognition analysis, which this learner would not stop to articulate under worksheet-copy pressure. The response should stay closer to the marked line and arrow key: name food/eater, maybe hesitate about setting the key aside, and show the rule working unevenly rather than summarizing the learning relationship.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the feedback is right: the response should stay procedural and artifact-led.

FINAL:
[slides the arrow key below the marked line, then leaves it slightly off to the side] Food first, eater second, energy follows the arrow. For this mark, rabbit is food and hawk is eater, so `rabbit -> hawk` stays. I’ll try the next one without the key, but if the food label comes second, that arrow flips.

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [slides the arrow key below the marked line, then leaves it slightly off to the side] Food first, eater second, energy follows the arrow. For this mark, rabbit is food and hawk is eater, so `rabbit -> hawk` stays. I’ll try the next one without the key, but if the food label comes second, that arrow flips.
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [slides the arrow key down to the next marked line] The check is working: food first, eater second, energy follows the arrow. On this next mark, name food and eater first, then decide whether it flips. After that, set the key aside for one mark before it goes on the copy sheet.

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage: [sets the arrow key at the bench edge and lines the copy sheet beside the marked worksheet] That check holds. Run the last mark without the key: food first, eater second. If the food label comes second, flip it before it reaches the class copy.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets the arrow key at the bench edge and lines the copy sheet beside the marked worksheet] `rabbit -> hawk` stays: rabbit is food, hawk is eater, energy follows the arrow. Run the last no-key check now; any arrow with food second flips before this becomes the class copy.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It keeps the artifact in control by moving the arrow key to the bench edge and placing the copy sheet beside the marked worksheet, which makes the scaffold visibly temporary and raises the deadline stakes without dramatizing them. It also correctly marks what the learner can already do: `rabbit -> hawk` stays because rabbit is food, hawk is eater, and energy follows the arrow.

The main risk is that it slightly over-confirms the answer before handing off the final check. Since the learner already reasoned this through, repeating the full answer may flatten the learner’s agency. The stronger move is to keep the material setup and procedural deadline, but reduce the explanatory echo so the learner has to carry the last no-key check independently. Socratically, it does not need a broad generative question; this is a lab-skills closure, so a narrow procedural prompt is appropriate.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the bench-edge arrow key, copy sheet, food-to-eater rule, and last no-key check. Change only the amount of confirmation: avoid re-teaching the whole `rabbit -> hawk` logic after the learner has already supplied it, and leave slightly more of the final decision work with the learner.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the scaffold removal but avoid re-teaching the answer the learner already supplied.
FINAL:
[sets the arrow key at the bench edge and lines the copy sheet beside the marked worksheet] That check holds. Run the last mark without the key: food first, eater second. If the food label comes second, flip it before it reaches the class copy.

lessons.concept: Why a food-web arrow records energy transfer, not liking or chasing
